video_title: Tokenized Assets Hit $15 Billion (Here's What's Next) | Ian de Bode
gist: Ian de Bode argues tokenized securities are scaling fast because they are structured as permissionless wrappers (debt instruments backed by real stock) rather than native shares, mirroring how stablecoins succeeded. He sees the next leg coming from using tokenized stocks as DeFi collateral and perpetual-swap collateral, with offshore distribution platforms and forthcoming US regulation as the key drivers.

themes.theme: Tokenized asset market sizing and growth

insights.claim: Tokenized treasuries grew from roughly $1 billion two years ago to nearly $15 billion, signaling explosive category-wide adoption of tokenized real-world assets.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Confirms tokenized RWAs are an exponentially growing addressable market; track treasury TVL as the leading indicator of on-chain capital formation.
insights.quote: Two years ago, that category as a whole stood at $1 billion. Right now, we're coming up on $15 billion

insights.claim: Tokenized stocks and ETFs were only brought on-chain around June of the prior year and have already crossed roughly $1.5 billion, making it the fastest-emerging tokenized asset class.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: A new asset class going from zero to $1.5B in under a year suggests durable demand; early infrastructure and issuers in this niche could capture outsized share.
insights.quote: tokenized stocks and ETFs that as an asset class was really just brought on chain last year in June as a starter and already we're starting to cross a billion and a half

insights.claim: On-chain capital that originated in stablecoins is now actively rotating into other tokenized assets, with infrastructure buildout largely complete, removing the main bottleneck to further asset migration on-chain.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Implies the supply of tokenizable assets, not demand or infrastructure, is now the binding constraint; issuers expanding asset coverage benefit first.

themes.theme: Distribution: offshore demand and the everything-app race

insights.claim: Demand for tokenized stocks is almost entirely outside the US, driven by large crypto exchanges and wallets such as Binance, OKX, Bitget, Gate, MetaMask and Phantom listing them.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: The growth market is offshore retail gaining first-time seamless access to US equities; US-only platforms miss this flow entirely.
insights.quote: all of that is outside of the US, right? So, our assets are actually listed on Binance, on OKX, on BitGate, Gate, Minamask, Phantom

insights.claim: Crypto distribution platforms are racing to become 'everything apps' modeled on Robinhood, bundling crypto, stocks, ETFs and prediction markets into a single interface.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Watch exchanges adding equities and prediction markets; the strategic value accrues to whoever owns the unified retail front-end.
insights.quote: Everyone's looking at Robin Hood. They want to be Robin Hood. What does Robin Hood mean? It means, you know, crypto, but also stocks, ETFs, prediction markets, all of these things.

insights.claim: It is operationally easier for crypto platforms to support stocks in tokenized form because they can reuse existing crypto custody and accounting infrastructure, keep markets open 24/7, and settle in stablecoins.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Explains why tokenized equities, not traditional brokerage rails, are the path of least resistance for crypto-native distributors; tailwind for tokenized over conventional listings offshore.
insights.quote: With tokenized stocks, they can use their existing custody infrastructure, their existing accounting systems. They can keep the market open 24/7 and you can do stable coin settlements on these things

themes.theme: Tokenization structure: wrappers vs native issuance

insights.claim: Native tokenization (where the token is the actual share) has failed to attract buyers because it requires issuer consent, leaving only about five companies available, mostly crypto-native or struggling firms seeking distribution.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Bets on native-stock tokenization models face an adverse-selection and supply problem; the wrapper approach is structurally favored for blue-chip exposure.
insights.quote: if you look at the numbers, no one's really buying those things

insights.claim: Native tokenized shares require KYC with a transfer agent and wallet whitelisting, so they cannot be freely transferred or used in DeFi, effectively recreating a brokerage account with fewer stocks.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: The permissioned constraints strip away the composability that gives tokenization its value; a key reason to discount native-issuance plays.
insights.quote: really what you're doing is you're kind of reinventing a brokerage account, a normal investing account, but with less stocks and that's it

insights.claim: The favored model issues permissionless wrappers structured as debt instruments against an offshore issuer that buys and custodies the underlying stock, giving holders full economic exposure plus a perfected security interest enforced by an independent third party.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: This debt-wrapper-plus-third-party-attestation structure is the template enabling transferability and DeFi use; evaluate any tokenized-equity issuer on whether collateral verification is genuinely independent.
insights.quote: Ours are debt instruments against the issuer that you can hold and it represents full economic exposure to the underlying.

insights.claim: An independent third party with access to the issuer's brokerage accounts posts daily attestations of backing and holds legal authority to seize and liquidate collateral to repay token holders if the issuer fails to meet redemptions.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Daily independent attestation plus enforceable seizure rights is the credible investor-protection bar; absence of it is a red flag for tokenized security risk.
insights.quote: that third party has legal authority to claim all of the assets in the brokerage account, sell them for cash, repay all the debt holders, which are the token holders that are on chain

insights.claim: Tokenized stocks, ETFs and treasuries are best understood as an extension of stablecoins: permissionless wrappers giving economic exposure to an asset backed by real collateral, which is precisely why they work in DeFi and distribution.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Frames the entire tokenized-securities thesis as stablecoin mechanics applied to new assets; the stablecoin adoption curve is the template for forecasting these assets.
insights.quote: Our tokenized stocks, ETFs, and treasuries are exactly like that. They're just an extension of stable coins.

insights.claim: Tokenized bank deposits, the true on-chain equivalent of native cash, have seen no adoption because they would be fully KYC'd and unusable in DeFi, demonstrating that permissionlessness is the decisive driver of on-chain asset adoption.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Bank-led tokenized deposit initiatives may struggle versus permissionless stablecoins; permissionlessness, not bank backing, predicts on-chain success.
insights.quote: No one's using tokenized bank deposits on chain in part because they would be fully KYC. You can't use it in DeFi.

themes.theme: Next leg: collateral, margin and prime brokerage on-chain

insights.claim: Tokenized stocks will scale meaningfully only once the on-chain version is strictly better than a brokerage account, which requires more stocks and ETFs on-chain plus the ability to get margin against them.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: The catalyst to watch is on-chain margin and broader asset coverage; until then growth stays niche relative to brokerage.
insights.quote: once your stock on chain is better than what you can have in a brokerage account, that's when this thing really scales

insights.claim: Because tokenized stocks can be freely transferred between wallets, holders can shop for margin rates across DeFi rather than being locked into a single broker's high rate, structurally pressuring brokerage margin economics.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: On-chain rate competition could compress brokerage margin-lending spreads; a long-term threat to incumbent broker revenue and an opportunity for DeFi lending venues.
insights.quote: With tokenized stocks, you can freely transfer these things around between wallets. You can go shop around rates for margin in DeFi.

insights.claim: Using tokenized stocks as collateral for perpetual swaps on those same names will dramatically improve capital efficiency and enable on-chain carry trades and prime-brokerage-style leverage previously unavailable in DeFi.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: A new perps platform letting tokenized stocks back same-name perps is the unlock to watch for capital-efficient equity hedging and carry on-chain.
insights.quote: The key innovation is going to be using your tokenized stocks as collateral for pers on those same names that's dramatically going to improve capital efficiency

insights.claim: Looping tokenized stocks as DeFi collateral replicates the 10x leverage hedge funds already obtain via spot margin in traditional finance, but with high-quality collateral like the S&P 500 and open globally to anyone with internet, stablecoins and the token.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Democratized equity leverage could expand the user base for looping strategies; quality of accepted collateral is the key risk differentiator versus crypto-native looping.
insights.quote: if you do a looping strategy on the S&P 500 that's more or less what a lot of funds are doing anyway right but now it's open to anyone globally with an internet connection

insights.claim: DeFi would benefit from a systemic backstop for important protocols, but unlike traditional central-bank bailouts where taxpayers foot the bill, DeFi backstops are collectively funded by protocols, making the model arguably fairer.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Protocol-funded mutual backstops may emerge as a structural feature; their existence could de-risk systemic DeFi and is a governance trend to track.
insights.quote: the difference here with DeFi and Trafi is when the Fed bills something out like they did in the global financial crisis is is the taxpayers that end up footing the bill whereas here in DeFi it's not

insights.claim: DeFi security is not just smart-contract audits and multisigs but fundamentally about protocol design and what is accepted as collateral, so bringing high-quality tokenized stocks and ETFs in as collateral should make these systems safer.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Collateral quality is an under-appreciated DeFi risk axis; protocols accepting tokenized blue-chip equities may prove more resilient than those reliant on volatile crypto collateral.
insights.quote: when you think about security, it isn't just, you know, multi-IGs and is my smart contract secure. It's also how you've designed the protocol. What do you accept as collateral?

insights.claim: DeFi yields are frequently not commensurate with the smart-contract risk taken, so investors earning low rates should hold tokenized treasuries instead of depositing into a smart contract.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: A practical allocation signal: benchmark any DeFi yield against tokenized treasuries and avoid uncompensated smart-contract risk.
insights.quote: the rates that people are getting are not commensurate with the risk and then quite frankly they should not be putting it into a smart contract. They should just put it in tokenized treasuries instead.

insights.claim: Tokenizing pre-IPO names like Anthropic, SpaceX and OpenAI is risky because illiquid private assets are easily mispriced upward on thin liquidity and may crater once they list publicly at far higher volume.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Tempers FOMO around tokenized pre-IPO exposure; the marked price may not survive an IPO, creating downside for retail buyers.
insights.quote: If this thing lists on the NASDAQ or the New York Stock Exchange with like 10 or 100x the volume and liquidity available, the price may crater

insights.claim: Private companies retain control over their cap table and can shut down unauthorized tokenization, leaving SPV-based buyers exposed to not actually holding the asset they thought they owned.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Counterparty and legal-title risk in tokenized private deals is acute; public stocks are structurally safer because issuers relinquish cap-table control at IPO.
insights.quote: A a large company all of a sudden figured it out and said, "Wait, what are you doing? You're not supposed to do that." And basically shut it down

insights.claim: Perpetual swaps on single-name equities, ETFs and commodities are a major untapped opportunity because perps are the preferred leverage instrument in crypto but unknown in traditional finance, where expiry-based futures dominate.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Cracking perps on stocks could create a market larger than crypto perps; watch venues like Hyperliquid and Trade XYZ extending perps to equities.
insights.quote: if you can if you can crack the carry trade or like perpetuals on stocks, that thing will be significantly larger in terms of market size than crypto

insights.claim: Perps offer dramatically lower fees and better capital efficiency than traditional options, where the fees an options trader pays around expiry are described as absurd.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Fee and capital-efficiency advantages of perps versus options favor on-chain perp venues for active and volatility traders.

insights.claim: True 24/7 trading of tokenized equities depends on weekend liquidity, which will arrive once NASDAQ and the NYSE move to 24/7 trading, publicly targeted as soon as this year, providing weekend price signals to anchor perps.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: A potential 2026 catalyst: major-exchange 24/7 trading could unlock weekend liquidity and perp markets for tokenized stocks; monitor NYSE/NASDAQ 24/7 rollout.
insights.quote: the NASDAQ and New York Stock Exchange have been very public about moving towards 24/7 trade ideally even this year

insights.claim: Major market dislocations can bootstrap weekend liquidity on-chain, as seen when the Iran crisis drove traders to onboard to Hyperliquid for real oil price signals when no other venue was open.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: On-chain perp venues can capture price discovery when traditional markets are closed during crises; a structural edge for 24/7 platforms during off-hours volatility.
insights.quote: That's what we saw with the Iran crisis and oil markets are hyperlquid

themes.theme: Regulation: GENIUS Act and market structure

insights.claim: The GENIUS Act has been approved but is not yet applicable, with rules being written and effect expected around November of this year, and it has already triggered a scramble among banks to respond to stablecoins.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: A November regulatory go-live is a near-term catalyst; banks entering stablecoins could reshape competitive dynamics for issuers.
insights.quote: the Genius Act has been approved but it's not yet applicable. It's later I think in November of this year

insights.claim: The GENIUS Act's main effect was signaling that stablecoins are permanent, driving rising institutional capital and TVL inflows now that a regulatory framework exists even before all rules are finalized.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Regulatory legitimacy, not finalized rules, is what unlocks institutional flows; expect similar front-running once tokenized-security frameworks signal permanence.
insights.quote: the big institutions know stable coins are here to stay. So they're they're they're flowing into it.

insights.claim: Market-structure legislation contains little specific to tokenized securities beyond affirming they are here to stay and granting the SEC full rulemaking authority, but that signal alone should pull capital in the way GENIUS did for stablecoins.
insights.speaker: Ian de Bode
insights.so_what: Expect a stablecoin-style adoption wave for tokenized securities on regulatory affirmation; SEC rulemaking is the next gating item to monitor.
insights.quote: You should prepare for a world in which tokenized securities coexist with normal securities, so get ready.
